Overweight positions in shares of high-P/E names like Wingstop (P/E 91.3x), Intuitive Surgical (65.4x), Palo Alto Networks (59.9x), and Fair Isaac (61.1x) push valuation higher, offsetting the lack of exposure to other high multiple companies like Tesla, Amazon, Palantir, and Costco. Overall, FQAL’s valuation is consistent with its core exposure to companies like Microsoft, NVIDIA, Apple, and Alphabet, which, to a large extent, mirror equity index benchmarks’ allocation.

The fund shows a balanced growth profile. Historical earnings growth of 10.5% outpaces the Russell 1000’s 7.4%, while long-term earnings and sales growth are in line with the benchmark. This reflects strong growth in healthcare, driven by holdings in IDEXX Laboratories (3Y EPS growth 75.2) and Veeva Systems (31.3%), rivaling high-growth names from technology such as NVIDIA, KLA, and Salesforce. On the downside, modest growth in key holdings in consumer discretionary, such as Yum! Brands, Home Depot, and Autozone, but also in consumer staples like P&G and Philip Morris, are detractors for FQAL’s aggregate growth figures.

Profitability is one of FQAL’s bright spots. The fund’s EBITDA margin of over 40% is well above the Russell 1000's 33.7%, while its ROE of 54.6% is 11.7 percentage points higher than the benchmark’s 42.9%. The fund shows stronger margins across all sectors, with key contributions coming from technology, financial services, consumer discretionary, and staples. At the stock level, FQAL benefits from overweight positions in McDonald’s (EBITDA margin 54.1%), CME Group (70.4%), NVIDIA (57.9%), Colgate-Palmolive (ROE 451.3%), Mastercard (187.7%), and KLA (104.1%). Meanwhile, its lack of exposure to low-margin companies like Amazon (EBITDA margin 19.4%, ROE 25.2%) and Tesla (EBITDA margin 13.1%, ROE 8.8%) reinforces FQAL’s strong selective approach when it comes to profitability.

FQAL’s valuation sits at the higher end of the peer group, as funds from JQUA to QUAL show valuations in the range of 21.7x to 23.7x, highlighting a similar sector allocation profile. However, FQAL carries a significant valuation premium to VFQY and ROE. Differences in market cap allocation help explain this valuation gap: While FQAL maintains nearly 75% allocated to large- and mega-caps, ROE keeps 44% in mid caps, and VFQY has 65% of the fund in mid and small caps. This heavy exposure to smaller companies, which typically trade at a discount relative to large caps, plays a key role in weighing down these two funds’ valuation relative to their counterparts.

Performance Not Keeping Pace With Benchmark And Peers

Quality-oriented ETFs have done well over time, with annualized returns of 16.0% over the past 5 years and 18.6% over the past 3 years on average, marginally outpacing the Russell 1000 index, driven by strong gains from SPHQ, JQUA, and VFQY. However, the same can not be said about FQAL, which has lagged behind the benchmark by roughly 1% and all peers. FQAL delivered weaker returns in most years, including the bearish year of 2022, with the exception of the year 2021, when the world economy was exiting the pandemic, and in 2024, a year when the fund performed in line with peers.

But it is fair to say that FQAL has been a less volatile investment relative to the broader market. Its beta of 0.97 is a few percentage points below the Russell 1000 and has been the second lowest among peers, together with SPHQ, but still higher than JQUA, the least volatile fund with a beta of 0.93. FQAL’s participation of 96% of the upside and 99% of the downside moves during the 5-year period tells us a little more about the fund profile. It tends to be a more stable investment than the broader market, but has trailed during bullish environments. That has been a key detractor to performance in an overall positive period for equities in the past decade or so.

FQAL’s average daily volume of $3.97 M is modest and much lower compared to most peers, including those in the middle of the pack, such as DUHP ($38.58M) and JQUA ($41.10M). This is reflected in the FQAL’s average spread of 0.13%, which is far above spreads in the range of 0.02% to 0.04% seen in top peers. FQAL’s expense ratio of 0.16% is in line with the peer group, and is consistent with a moderate turnover of 34%. Meanwhile, the fund’s dividend yield of 1.22% is marginally higher than the peer group average and close to broader equity indexes. FQAL’s tracking error is the lowest in the group, highlighting a strong correlation with the underlying Fidelity U.S. Quality Factor Index. Lastly, its 3- and 5-year Sharpe ratios of 0.56 and 0.76, respectively, are similar to peers' average, but sit well below those of top peers like SPHQ (70 / 87) and JQUA (68 / 85), as these two funds have delivered a combination of strong gains and lower volatility. This is something that FQAL only partially achieves, as it trails SPHQ and JQUA in both measures.

Final Thoughts

There is no question that FQAL offers a portfolio with a high-quality tilt. After all, fundamentals look very solid, as evidenced by the fund’s high EBITDA margins and ROE. This is not an easy feat, especially when sector allocation remains tied to broader equity indexes like the Russell 1000. But this has not translated into stronger returns as FQAL has failed to outperform the benchmark and most peers over time.

It is not to say that FQAL has not generated healthy gains over the years. Even trailing the broader market, its annualized total returns of over 15% are decent by any measure if we consider historical standards. However, we must admit that expectations are high for quality funds, as investors in general see such investments as an opportunity for upside while also offering resilience during uncertain times – a playbook that appears quite appealing at this moment, with softening but still positive economic growth amid trade and geopolitical uncertainties.

That said, while FQAL could very well remain a long-term position for most investors who feel comfortable holding a portfolio with strong fundamentals and below-average volatility, those looking for outperformance may look elsewhere for better opportunities in this highly competitive category of quality ETFs.
